content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 15, 1995


John Wolfenden
6020 South Shingle Road
Shingle Springs, CA 95682

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-033

Dear Mr. Wolfenden:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities as an El Dorado County Planning
Commissioner under the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Do you have a conflict of interest in decisions regarding a
recreational vehicle park that was the subject of litigation
between the county and a nonprofit organization, the El Dorado
Council, of which you were formerly a member and chairman?

CONCLUSION

You do not have an economic interest in decisions affecting
the recreational vehicle park or the El Dorado Council.

_____________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-95-033
Page 2


FACTS

You provided the following facts:

     • Prior to 1993, you were involved as a member of the El
Dorado Council (the "council"), an unincorporated association of
home owner groups throughout the county.

     • In 1991, the council and another organization, Save Our
Rural Environment, filed suit against the Board of Supervisors of
El Dorado County regarding the zoning of a recreational vehicle
park in the county.  Your residence is more than five miles from
the park, and you do not have any interests in property near the
park.

     You stated that the lawsuit was funded by the home owner
groups directly.  You stated that the council did not have a
treasury or collect dues.  You also stated that you never donated
money to the lawsuit and are not entitled to any share of money
awarded to the council in the lawsuit.

     • In 1992, you were selected as the chairman for the
council.  In our January 23, 1995, telephone conversation you
stated that the position was noncompensated.  As chairman, on one
occasion, you accepted a check from the county in payment for an
award of sanctions and signed the check over to the attorneys
handling the litigation for the council.

     • In January 1993, you were appointed to the El Dorado
County Planning Commission.  Upon your appointment to the planning
commission, you left the council and all the other community
groups to devote your time to the planning commission.

     • In July 1994, the council won the case on appeal.

     • In response to the litigation, the board of supervisors
will be considering the modification of the general plan to create
a "tourist recreation" land use designation and place the
recreational vehicle park within the designation.  You started
that the planning commission will be considering a zoning change
to permit recreational vehicle parks in the RF zoning category.

ANALYSIS

     Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.  A "public official" is
defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural
person who is a member, officer, employee, or consultant of a
state or local government agency.  This definition would include a
member of the El Dorado County Planning Commission.  (See, Section
87200.)

Our File No. A-95-033
Page 3


     Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

     (a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

     (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

     (e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made. The amount of the value of
gifts specified by this subdivision shall be
adjusted biennially by the Commission to equal the
same amount determined by the Commission pursuant
to subdivision (d) of Section 89504.

     You have become concerned that your prior involvement with
the council might result in a conflict of interest with respect to
the zoning decisions coming before the planning commission.

     Section 87103: You would have a conflict of interest in any
decision that would materially affect you or your immediate
family. However, you stated that you will receive no payments as
a result of the litigation or of the land use change pertaining to
the park.

     Section 87103(a) and (d): A nonprofit association is not
considered a "business entity" under the Act. Section 82005 of
the Act defines a "business entity" as any organization or
enterprise operated for profit, including but not limited to a
proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture,

Our File No. A-95-033
Page 4




syndicate, corporation or association.  Thus, the association is
not a potentially disqualifying economic interest pursuant to
Section 87103(a) or (d).

     Section 87103(b): You stated your real property is five
miles from the park site.  Thus, it does not appear foreseeable
that your real property will be materially affected.  (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198; Regulation 18702.3(b).)

     Section 87103(c) and (e): However, a nonprofit entity may be
a source of income or of gifts pursuant to Section 87103(c) and
(e).  In our January 23, 1995, telephone conversation you stated
you received no compensation from the council.  Assuming that you
have not received any income or gifts from the council in the past
12 months, you would not have a conflict of interest in decisions
affecting the council or the park.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.2

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        By:  John W. Wallace
                                             Counsel, Legal Division




2     Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
